content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 19, 1994

Robert E. Leidigh
Olson, Hagel, Fong, Leidigh, Waters & Fishburn
CapitolBank Center
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-94-150

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

This is in reply to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice I gave you on April 27, 1994, concerning whether
an organization doing slate mailers would be a candidate
controlled committee under the Political Reform Act.¹

Unless you advise us otherwise, we assume from your letter
that your clients are Los Angeles County Supervisor Gloria Molina,
Pat Bond, and Robert Hertzberg. Accordingly, under Section
83114(b), the immunity provided by this letter extends to those
persons.

QUESTION

Would the activities described below be appropriately
classified as those of a slate mailer organization and not a
candidate controlled committee?

CONCLUSION

The activities described below would be those of a slate
mailer organization and not of a candidate controlled committee.

FACTS

Ms. Pat Bond is a political consultant. She consults for a
number of candidates, among them Los Angeles County Supervisor
Gloria Molina. She has been a political consultant for Supervisor

_________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-94-150
Page 2


Molina dating back to 1982. Robert Hertzberg is a Los Angeles
area attorney in private practice. He has been a political
supporter of Supervisor Molina, does legal work for her husband,
and has been appointed by her as a member of the Los Angeles
County Quality and Productivity Commission.

Supervisor Molina is up for election on June 7, 1994.
However, because of her popularity with her constituents, she is
unopposed. Supervisor Molina has just announced publicly a number
of candidates whom she has endorsed for election on June 7, 1994.

Ms. Bond and Mr. Hertzberg have formed a partnership for the
purpose of producing a slate mailer to be distributed throughout
Los Angeles County. The partnership, under the name Good
Government Guide, would produce a slate mailer which will endorse
those candidates endorsed by Supervisor Molina. It will state
very clearly that these are Ms. Molina's endorsements. The slate
mailer will have a heading stating either "Supervisor Gloria
Molina's Democratic Ballot Recommendations" or "Democratic Ballot
Guide from Supervisor Gloria Molina."

The partnership will file and report its activities as a
slate mailer organization, pursuant to the requirements of the
Political Reform Act. Appearance of the endorsees on the slate
will be sold to each endorsee. The rate for each endorsee will be
the same, based upon the number of pieces on which the endorsee
will appear. However, there will be a minimum fee to cover extra
postal charges for candidates who will appear on only a small
number of pieces. Not all endorsees will be on the ballot county-
wide; hence, not all endorsees will pay the same dollar amount,
since the price will vary by number of pieces, but the formula
will be the same.

For business reasons, like any other slate mailer, the slate
mailer organization may ultimately include one or more endorsees
without charge or at a reduced rate in order to complete its
slate. However, the decision to do this will be entirely Ms.
Bond's and Mr. Hertzberg's decision and the partnership will
bear the financial loss or reap the financial gain from the
selling of space on the slate mailer. Neither Supervisor Molina
nor her committee will receive any funds from the slate mailer
organization. Supervisor Molina will have no direct personal
involvement in the production of the slate mailer. She will not
participate in the decisions regarding design, numbers of pieces,
selection of vendors, etc. Nor will she be involved in soliciting
the endorsees to buy onto the slate. Those activities will be
performed by the partnership.

If the partnership decides to include an endorsement of
Supervisor Molina on the pieces sent within her own supervisorial
district, Supervisor Molina's committee will be required to pay
the "going rate" for the number of pieces on which such an
endorsement would appear, based upon the aforementioned formula.

File No. A-94-150
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Section 82048.4(a) defines slate mailer organization as
follows:

"Slate mailer organization" means, except as provided in
subdivision (b), any person who, directly or indirectly,
does all of the following:

(1) Is involved in the production of one or more
slate mailers and exercises control over the selection
of the candidates and measures to be supported or
opposed in the slate mailers.
(2) Receives or is promised payments totaling five
hundred dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year for
the production of one or more slate mailers.

According to subdivision (b) of Section 82048.4, a slate
mailer organization does not include a candidate's or
officeholder's controlled committee.

Section 82016 defines controlled committee as:

[A] committee which is controlled directly or indirectly
by a candidate or state measure proponent or which acts
jointly with a candidate, controlled committee or state
measure proponent in connection with the making of
expenditures. A candidate or state measure proponent
controls a committee if he, his agent or any other
committee he controls has a significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the committee.

From the facts presented, Supervisor Molina is a candidate²
and we will presume that she knew of Ms. Bond's and Mr.
Hertzberg's intention to use her publicly announced endorsements
in their planned mailers. Though the facts do not indicate one
way or another, given Ms. Bond's position as Supervisor Molina's
political consultant and Mr. Hertzberg's close political
association with Supervisor Molina, it is even likely that the
three of them discussed this matter. Thus, the issue is whether
Supervisor Molina's involvement in the selection of endorsements
to be used by Ms. Bond's and Mr. Hertzberg's mailers makes that
operation a candidate committee controlled by Supervisor Molina.

The Commission has advised that a candidate or elected
officer who is a voting member of a committee's board of directors
is presumed to be a controlling candidate, since the candidate or
officeholder probably exercises "significant influence on the

2 Section 82007 defines candidate, in pertinent part, to include
an individual, such as Supervisor Molina, who is listed on the
ballot for nomination or election to any elective office.

File No. A-94-150
Page 4


actions or decisions of the committee." (Ferguson Advice Letter,
No. A-86-044.)

However, in the Maupin Advice Letter, No. I-92-389, the
Commission was asked whether a candidate(in that case also an
elected officeholder like Supervisor Molina) could serve on a body
that determined what candidates or measures would be endorsed in
mailings by a state mailer organization. We concluded that this
was possible and did not convert the slate mailer organization
into the candidate's controlled committee unless the slate mailer
organization received unearmarked payments to help defray the
general cost of publishing the slate mailer.3 In that case,
unearmarked payments totalling $1,000 or more would convert the
slate mailer organization to a "committee" (see section 82003(a))
and the candidate's involvement in its governing body would create
a presumption that the candidate controlled the committee.

The rationale applied in the Maupin Letter likewise would
apply here. Supervisor Molina's interaction with Ms. Bond and
Mr. Hertzberg in their use of her endorsements on the slate
mailers does not, by virtue of that interaction, convert their
slate mailer organization into her controlled committee. This
would occur only if the slate mailer organization receives
unearmarked payments totalling $1,000 or more to help defray the
general cost of publishing the slate mailer. Otherwise, the slate
mailer organization remains a slate mailer organization and no
additional duties attach to the organization or Supervisor Molina
under the Act.4




3  As indicated in Maupin, supra, a payment to a slate mailer
organization is not normally classified as a "contribution" to a
"committee" because it is presumed that the candidate or committee
making the payment is paying for the candidate's or committee's
own advertisement in the mailer and is thus receiving full and
adequate consideration therefor.

4  We also note that the alternative headings to be used in the
slate mailer do not change the nature of the mailer into a
contribution to Supervisor Molina. Both headings essentially
highlight the fact that the slate listings are endorsements by
Supervisor Molina. When one candidate pays to advertise another
candidate's endorsement of him or her, there is no contribution to
the endorsing candidate. (See Wax v. Fair Political Practices
Commission, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of California,
Case No. CIVS 90-1232 LKK-JFM.)

File No. A-94-150
Page 5


Should you have further questions, please contact me at
916/322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By Scott Hallabrin
Assistant General Counsel

SGC:SH:ak

mailer organization and not of a candidate controlled committee.

QUESTION: Would the activities described below be appropriately
classified as those of a slate mailer organization and not a
candidate controlled committee?

CONCLUSION: The activities described below would be those of a slate
mailer organization and not of a candidate controlled committee.
